county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Mineral County formed 1893 from Hinsdale, Rio Grande, and Saguache counties. County seat is Creede. Last great silver boomtown (1890-1893). One of Colorado's least populated counties. Bob Ford (killed Jesse James) was murdered in Creede.
county_metadata.research_tips: Mineral County Clerk and Recorder in Creede holds records from 1893 (created from Hinsdale, Rio Grande, and Saguache counties). Creede was one of the last great silver mining boomtowns in Colorado (1890-1893), famous for its lawless early days (Bob Ford, who killed Jesse James, was murdered in Creede). One of the least populated counties in Colorado. The Creede Historical Society and Museum has mining-era collections. The Creede Candle newspaper documented the boomtown era. Check Hinsdale, Rio Grande, and Saguache counties for pre-1893 records. Colorado State Archives has county records on microfilm. BLM GLO mining claims document prospectors and miners.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Mineral County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Moffat County formed 1911 from Routt County. County seat is Craig. Named for David Moffat (railroad builder). Northwestern Colorado ranching. Coal development. Former Ute reservation until 1881.
county_metadata.research_tips: Moffat County Clerk and Recorder in Craig holds records from 1911 (created from Routt County). Northwestern Colorado ranching county with later coal mining development. Named for David Moffat, the railroad entrepreneur who built the Denver Northwestern & Pacific (Moffat Road). The Museum of Northwest Colorado in Craig has regional history collections including ranching, outlaw, and homesteading artifacts. Check Routt County for pre-1911 records. The county has Ute history and was part of the Ute reservation until 1881. BLM GLO records document homesteads. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. Church records in Craig document the small community. The Craig Empire Courier newspaper is a source for legal notices and vital records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Moffat County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Montezuma County formed 1889 from La Plata County. County seat is Cortez. Mesa Verde National Park (Ancestral Puebloan). Ute Mountain Ute Reservation. Ranching and farming. Mormon settlement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Montezuma County Clerk and Recorder in Cortez holds records from 1889 (created from La Plata County). Home to Mesa Verde National Park, one of the most significant archaeological sites in North America (Ancestral Puebloan cliff dwellings). The Ute Mountain Ute Reservation covers the southern portion of the county -- tribal records at the tribal office may be relevant for Ute families. Cortez Cultural Center and Anasazi Heritage Center (BLM) have archaeological and historical collections. Anglo-American settlement began in the 1880s with ranching and farming. Check La Plata County for pre-1889 records. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. BLM GLO records document land patents. Mormon settlers from Utah established some communities in the county.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Montezuma County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Montrose County formed 1883 from Gunnison County. County seat is Montrose. Ute homeland until 1881 removal. Uncompahgre Valley irrigation project (Bureau of Reclamation, 1909). Ute Indian Museum. Agriculture and ranching.
county_metadata.research_tips: Montrose County Clerk and Recorder holds records from 1883 (created from Gunnison County). The county was Ute homeland until removal in 1881. Montrose became an agricultural center with the Uncompahgre Valley irrigation project (one of the earliest US Bureau of Reclamation projects, 1909). The Montrose County Historical Society Museum has local collections. The Ute Indian Museum in Montrose (on the site of Chief Ouray's farm) documents Ute history and is relevant for families with Ute connections. Check Gunnison County for pre-1883 records. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. BLM GLO records document homesteads. The Montrose Daily Press newspaper is a source for vital records and legal notices. Church records from various denominations serve the farming communities.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Montrose County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Morgan County formed 1889 from Weld County. County seat is Fort Morgan. Named for Colonel Christopher Morgan. Military post origin (1865). Sugar beet farming. German-Russian, Hispanic, and Japanese immigrants. Glenn Miller's hometown.
county_metadata.research_tips: Morgan County Clerk and Recorder in Fort Morgan holds records from 1889 (created from Weld County). Fort Morgan was originally a military post (1865) and became a railroad and sugar beet farming town. Great Western Sugar Company records document immigrant workers -- German-Russians from the Volga region were particularly prominent, along with Hispanic and Japanese laborers. The Fort Morgan Museum has local history collections including sugar beet industry materials. Glenn Miller (musician) grew up in Fort Morgan. Check Weld County for pre-1889 records. BLM GLO homestead records are essential. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. Church records (Lutheran for German-Russians, Catholic for Hispanic families) are important supplements.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Morgan County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Otero County formed 1889 from Bent County. County seat is La Junta. Named for Miguel Otero. Santa Fe Railroad division point. Sugar beet farming. Bent's Old Fort National Historic Site. Koshare Indian Museum.
county_metadata.research_tips: Otero County Clerk and Recorder in La Junta holds records from 1889 (created from Bent County). La Junta was a Santa Fe Railroad division point and sugar beet farming center. Bent's Old Fort National Historic Site is in the county (but the fort's original records predate the county). The Koshare Indian Museum in La Junta has Native American and regional collections. Sugar beet farming brought German-Russian, Hispanic, and Japanese immigrant workers. The Amache (Granada) Japanese American internment camp (1942-1945) was nearby (technically in Crowley County). Check Bent County for pre-1889 records. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. BLM GLO homestead records. The La Junta Tribune-Democrat newspaper. Church records (Catholic, Methodist, Lutheran) serve diverse communities.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Otero County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Ouray County formed 1877 from Hinsdale, Lake, and San Juan counties. County seat is Ouray. 'Switzerland of America.' Silver and gold mining. Camp Bird Mine (Hope Diamond connection). Diverse immigrant mining communities.
county_metadata.research_tips: Ouray County Clerk and Recorder holds records from 1877 (created from Hinsdale, Lake, and San Juan counties). Known as the 'Switzerland of America,' Ouray was a silver and gold mining center with spectacular mountain scenery. The Bachelor-Syracuse and Camp Bird mines were among the most productive. Ouray County Historical Society Museum has excellent mining-era collections. Thomas Walsh (owner of the Hope Diamond) made his fortune at the Camp Bird Mine. The county attracted diverse mining workers including Cornish, Finnish, Italian, and Scandinavian immigrants. Check Hinsdale, Lake, and San Juan counties for pre-1877 records. Colorado State Archives has county records on microfilm. BLM GLO mining claims and patents. The Ouray County Plaindealer newspaper (from 1877) is a rich source for mining-era families.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Ouray County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Park County formed 1861 as one of the original 17 territorial counties. County seat is Fairplay. South Park gold mining. Denver South Park & Pacific Railroad. Ghost towns (Alma, Como, Tarryall). One of the largest counties by area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Park County Clerk and Recorder in Fairplay holds records from 1861 as an original territorial county. South Park was a major gold mining area in the 1859-1860s Pike's Peak gold rush. Fairplay, Alma, Como, and Tarryall were mining camps. South Park City Museum in Fairplay preserves a reconstructed mining town with period artifacts. The Denver South Park & Pacific Railroad (narrow gauge) connected Park County to Denver. Park County had diverse mining communities. The county is one of the largest by area in Colorado. Colorado State Archives has early territorial and county records on microfilm. BLM GLO mining claims and homestead patents. The Flume newspaper (Fairplay) has been published since 1879. Church records from the scattered mining communities.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Park County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Phillips County formed 1889 from Logan County. County seat is Holyoke. Named for R.O. Phillips (secretary of Lincoln Land Company). Northeastern plains farming. Burlington Railroad. German-Russian settlers.
county_metadata.research_tips: Phillips County Clerk and Recorder in Holyoke holds records from 1889 (created from Logan County). A northeastern plains county with farming and ranching heritage. Holyoke was a Burlington Railroad town. The county had significant homesteading activity in the 1890s-1910s. German-Russian settlers established farming communities. Phillips County Museum in Holyoke has local collections. Check Logan County for pre-1889 records. BLM GLO homestead records document original settlers. Colorado State Archives has microfilmed county records. Church records (Lutheran, Catholic) are important for German-Russian families. The Holyoke Enterprise newspaper is a source for vital records and legal notices.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Phillips County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Pitkin County formed 1881 from Gunnison County. County seat is Aspen. Named for Governor Frederick Pitkin. Silver mining boom (1880s-1890s) followed by bust and rebirth as world-class ski resort. Diverse mining-era immigrant communities.
county_metadata.research_tips: Pitkin County Clerk and Recorder in Aspen holds records from 1881 (created from Gunnison County). Aspen was one of the world's great silver mining towns in the 1880s-1890s before the 1893 silver crash devastated the economy. The town nearly died before being reborn as a ski resort (1946) and cultural center. Aspen Historical Society has excellent mining-era and resort-era collections including photographs, oral histories, and manuscript materials. The Wheeler Opera House records. Diverse immigrant miners included Cornish, Swedish, Austrian, and Italian workers. The Aspen Daily Times newspaper (from 1881) documents the mining era. Check Gunnison County for pre-1881 records. Colorado State Archives has county records on microfilm. BLM GLO mining claims.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM General Land Office records for Pitkin County; federal land patents (homesteads, mining claims, timber culture entries) searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov

source_profiles.source_name: Pitkin County Federal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census for Pitkin County from 1890; 1885 Colorado state census is an important supplement; check parent county for earlier census records

source_profiles.source_name: Pitkin County Mining District Records and Claim Filings
source_profiles.repository: Colorado State Archives / Pitkin County Clerk and Recorder / BLM
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Mining district minutes, claim records, assessment work filings, and mining company records for Pitkin County; mining districts kept their own records before formal county recording; Colorado State Archives and History Colorado hold many mining-era records

source_profiles.source_name: Ute Nation Records -- Pitkin County Area
source_profiles.repository: NARA, Denver / Southern Ute Indian Tribe / Ute Mountain Ute Tribe / History Colorado
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Pre-removal Ute records for the Pitkin County area including treaty records, agency records, annuity rolls, and correspondence; Ute removal from western Colorado completed by 1881; Southern Ute and Ute Mountain Ute reservations in southwestern Colorado retain tribal records; NARA Denver holds federal Indian agency records

source_profiles.source_name: Pitkin County Historic Newspapers -- Colorado Historic Newspapers Collection
source_profiles.repository: Colorado State Library / Chronicling America / Colorado Historic Newspapers Collection
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Digitized historic newspapers for Pitkin County communities; Colorado Historic Newspapers Collection (CHNC) provides free online access to many Colorado papers; obituaries, legal notices, marriage and death announcements, estate notices, and community news
